script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Tuesday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed four point six five percent down on the session. It was a broad, punishing selloff across every primary position, with not a single name finishing in the green. The damage was sector-wide and, in some cases, severe.

Geopolitics lit the fuse. President Donald Trump announced the United States would resume a blockade of Iranian ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz — and then went further, claiming the right to impose a twenty percent toll on all vessel traffic through one of the world's most critical waterways. Oil prices surged. Risk assets sold off hard. Semiconductor equities, already carrying elevated valuations, absorbed the worst of it.

Chip stocks fell broadly on Tuesday as Middle East tensions reignited risk-off sentiment across equity markets. The Nasdaq ended the session meaningfully lower, dragged down by technology and semiconductor names. No major domestic macro data prints were on the calendar, leaving geopolitical headlines as the dominant driver of price action through the close.

The single most consequential development for this portfolio on Tuesday is the split emerging between logic and memory demand inside the AI hardware supply chain. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company reported a blowout quarter — revenue jumping thirty-six percent — confirming that demand for advanced logic chips, the kind powering artificial intelligence accelerators and next-generation processors, remains structurally robust. But memory stocks cratered in the same session. The divergence matters because it signals that AI hardware spending is concentrating around compute and custom silicon, while memory appears to be entering a softer patch. For this portfolio, which holds both sides of that divide, the read-through is nuanced. Names tied tightly to foundry logic — like Advanced Micro Devices and Nvidia, whose most advanced chips are built through Taiwan Semiconductor's facilities — benefit from that demand signal. Names with heavier memory exposure face a more complicated near-term setup. Analysts flagged Tuesday's rotation out of chip stocks and into enterprise software as a profit-taking move, not a fundamental reversal, but the memory-versus-logic split is a dynamic worth monitoring closely into the next earnings cycle.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down four point three two percent on the session. Micron is the portfolio's largest holding at eight point five four percent, and Tuesday's move landed it squarely in the middle of the memory selloff narrative. The broader context here includes ASML's earnings report due Wednesday — the Dutch lithography giant is expected to address capacity plans and exposure to China, where U.S. export controls are tightening. A proposed U.S. law would require allies to align with those controls, with ASML named in the legislation directly. ASML has denied selling its most advanced extreme ultraviolet tools to China, but China is forecast to account for up to twenty percent of ASML's sales this year through legal purchases of less-advanced deep ultraviolet equipment. How ASML frames that exposure Wednesday morning will set the tone for the equipment names across this portfolio.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point two one percent. The company's most advanced chips are manufactured at Taiwan Semiconductor's facilities, and Tuesday's strong foundry revenue report is a constructive signal for Advanced Micro Devices' supply chain. The broader macro headwind from the Hormuz situation weighed on the stock alongside the sector, but the foundry data point provides some fundamental ballast.

Nvidia closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, down three point five two percent. That made it the best relative performer among the top three holdings on Tuesday. Like Advanced Micro Devices, Nvidia's flagship processors flow through Taiwan Semiconductor, and the foundry's blowout quarter speaks directly to sustained demand for Nvidia's compute products at the manufacturing level. The session's decline appears driven by macro pressure rather than company-specific deterioration.

Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down six point one two percent — one of the sharper single-session moves in the primary lineup. Intel was specifically named in Tuesday's broad market selloff coverage tied to U.S.-Iran tensions and the Hormuz situation, alongside other large-cap technology names. Intel's position in this portfolio at six point three three percent means that kind of drawdown carries weight. The company continues to navigate its own manufacturing transition, and geopolitical noise adds another layer of uncertainty around its foundry ambitions.

Broadcom closed at three hundred eighty-four dollars and five cents, down three point nine eight percent. The session's decline comes against what is otherwise a positive fundamental backdrop. In early July, Broadcom announced an expanded collaboration with Apple through twenty thirty-one, covering custom application-specific integrated circuit silicon across multiple generations of Apple products. Analysts have noted that this long-dated contract, combined with Broadcom's growing artificial intelligence semiconductor business and deepening cloud relationships, reinforces its role as a core infrastructure supplier. Tuesday's move looks macro-driven.

Applied Materials closed at five hundred seventy-five dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point five zero percent. As a capital equipment supplier, Applied Materials is exposed to the same China export control dynamics flagged in the ASML headlines. ASML's earnings Wednesday will serve as a direct read-through for Applied Materials' own demand picture heading into its next report.

KLA Corporation closed at two hundred twenty-two dollars and twenty-five cents, down four point zero zero percent. KLA's decline tracked the sector closely on Tuesday, with no company-specific catalyst. Like Applied Materials, it is a process control equipment name, and the ASML earnings event Wednesday carries direct relevance.

Lam Research closed at three hundred twenty-nine dollars and ninety-two cents, down five point eight three percent. That is one of the steeper single-day moves in the portfolio on Tuesday, exceeded only by Marvell Technology. Lam Research is a deposition and etch equipment supplier with meaningful exposure to memory customers, placing it at the intersection of both headwinds active on Tuesday — the memory demand softness and the tightening China export environment.

Marvell Technology closed at two hundred seventeen dollars and fifty-three cents, down seven point seven five percent — the worst single-session performance among all ten primary holdings on Tuesday. No company-specific headline drove the move, but Marvell's heavy exposure to custom artificial intelligence silicon and data center networking makes it sensitive to any shift in AI spending confidence. The magnitude of Tuesday's decline, in the absence of direct news, suggests the stock absorbed outsized macro and sector-rotation pressure.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company closed at four hundred twenty-one dollars and fifty-eight cents, down two point eight nine percent — the most resilient primary holding in Tuesday's session. The company reported revenue growth of thirty-six percent in its latest quarter, driven by sustained demand for advanced logic chips powering artificial intelligence hardware. That result is the clearest positive fundamental data point to emerge from Tuesday's session for this portfolio.

Three events in the next forty-eight hours are worth tracking closely. First, ASML reports quarterly earnings Wednesday morning — the results and guidance will shape the outlook for every equipment name in this portfolio, including Applied Materials, KLA Corporation, and Lam Research. Second, watch for any further developments on the Strait of Hormuz situation and potential coalition responses to Trump's toll announcement, as escalation would add additional pressure to risk assets. Third, monitor Taiwan Semiconductor's management commentary from its earnings call for any forward guidance on capacity allocation, customer concentration, and the impact of U.S. export restrictions on its China-facing business.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. ASML's earnings are the single most important event on the horizon — what that company says about China, capacity, and the equipment cycle will set the narrative for the rest of the week.
